summary: Tight second‑set jams with standout Turn On Your Lovelight
blurb: The second set shines with tight unit playing and memorable solos, especially on Cumberland Blues and Turn On Your Lovelight. Jerry delivers inventive leads on Me And My Uncle and a fiery Johnny B. Goode. The soundboard recording captures the band at its best.
review: May 30 1971 at Winterland was a classic early‑70s outing that rewards fans with a powerhouse second set. After an average first set (only available via audience tape), the band erupts on songs like China Cat Sunflower → I Know You Rider, Sugar Magnolia and especially Cumberland Blues, where Phil Lesh’s bass is described as a “behemoth driving a tank.” Jerry Garcia’s lead work is on fire – inventive solos behind Bob Weir’s rhythm on Me And My Uncle, a scorching Johnny B. Goode, and an overall “Jer bein’ good” vibe throughout the latter half. The highlight of the night is Turn On Your Lovelight, which showcases tight unit playing and soaring solos before closing with Uncle John’s Band, Casey Jones, and a rousing encore of Johnny B. Goode. The Miller soundboard mix (gd1971-05-30.sbd.miller.94119.flac16) delivers crisp hi‑fi stereo that captures the energy of the second set despite some missing first‑set material and minor technical hiccups, making it the recommended listening source.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: One recording is clearly positive and the other mixed; overall sentiment leans positive but lacks unanimous praise, warranting a modest +0.6 adjustment.

best_recording.reason: Excellent soundboard mix with crisp hi‑fi stereo, captures the tight second set and standout solos; only minor drop‑outs and missing first‑set material.

key_highlights: Tight unit playing and stellar solos on the second set (Cumberland Blues, Turn On Your Lovelight)
Jerry Garcia’s inventive lead work on Me And My Uncle and a fiery Johnny B. Goode
Phil Lesh’s powerhouse bass on Cumberland Blues described as a ‘behemoth driving a tank’
Miller SBD recording offers the clearest audio of this era despite some missing first‑set portions

band_performance.Jerry: Strong lead work – inventive solo behind Bob on Me And My Uncle, fiery Johnny B. Goode, and overall ‘Jer bein’ good’ throughout the second set.
band_performance.Phil: Described as a ‘behemoth driving a tank’ on Cumberland Blues, anchoring a tight rhythm section.
band_performance.Bob: Provides solid rhythmic foundation; Jerry’s solo sits neatly behind his rhythm during Me And My Uncle.
